text: The electronic factors that control the sensitivity of CO-stretching modes to the metal-ligand environment in transition metal carbonyl compounds are investigated by combining charge-displacement analysis with density functional theory. The effect of the metal environment on the σ and π components of the coordination bond, as well as the corresponding π-polarization of the CO ligand in over thirty transition metal (mainly gold(I)) complexes has been explored, including homoleptic anionic, neutral and cationic complexes. By systematically comparing different metal fragments within the same ligand field and by comparing analogues with different ligand fields, the effects on the metal-CO σ-bond and the polarization of the CO ligand were separated. In the investigated series of gold(I) adducts, homoleptic anionic complexes, neutral metal tetracarbonyl compounds, and homoleptic cationic complexes the σ-donation of the CO ligand into the metal centre is quite constant, whereas the π-backbonding and thus the π-polarization of the CO ligand show a high sensitivity towards the metal centre and its ligand field. Moreover, a quantitative correlation is established between the π-backbonding and the changes in the CO-stretching frequency and bond-length, which is applicable to almost all the complexes under investigation. Even in non-classical cationic complexes, where the CO-bond is elongated, the π-backbonding remains an important competing factor to the electrostatic metal-CO repulsion. Thus, the CO-stretching frequency of a transition metal carbonyl complex is almost exclusively controlled by the π-polarization of the CO ligand.
